sentences.text: That I experienced personally, it did not go much further than what I've already told you about, but I saw all around me things that were happening as a result of the new laws and new decrees-- anti-Semitic decrees-- that came out from the government all the time.
It just never stopped.
Every month there was a new decree.
It started out with the schools, then it went to things like the swimming pools, as I told you.
Then it went to the movie theaters where at first there would be one or two where Jews were allowed to go.
The others had big signs on their box office, fur juden verboten, which meant forbidden for Jews.
So you didn't go in there.
Then it went to the coffee houses, which was a very popular German pastime.
Everybody went to have coffee and cake at 4:00 in the afternoon, my parents no exception.
And then they closed that down, and Jews were not allowed to go there.

And then a decree came out that any store owned by a Jewish proprietor had to have the proprietor's name painted on the store window in one-foot high letters-- actually about 50 centimeters-- very large-- and this identified the store as a Jewish store.
Jews were allowed to buy in any store they wanted.
That is one thing-- right they never took away, to spend the money and the non-Jewish stores.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Gentiles, when they went into a Jewish store, were hassled by the stormtroopers.

Usually there would be one or two loitering near those stores, and if they saw people that looked quote unquote 'Aryan' go into the store, meaning they were blond, they were blue eyed, they were not Jewish looking.
These stormtroopers would walk up to them and say don't you know that this is a Jewish store or what have you.
Very often they hit on tourists who didn't even speak German and had no clue of what was going on.
So that happened.
And then they would leave them alone.
But if it was a German person, they would ask for their ID papers and take their name and intimidate them.
They wouldn't-- I don't think they ever did anything about it.
But they would intimidate them, and these people would never do it again.
There were constant parades.
Every time that Hitler made a speech, he would assemble all the stormtroopers and the SS and part of the Army, and they would go into one of the big sports stadiums.
And Hitler would make a speech, and they would parade around town.
If it was night time, they would carry torches, and they would sing these marching songs.
And it was all very paramilitary.
Did I see Hitler?
Several times, yes.
I saw Hitler.
I saw Mussolini.
Seeing Hitler and parades in the street were a problem for the Jews because, like in the United States, when a flag passes, you are supposed to salute the flag.
Now, they were carrying the new German flag, the swastika, and
It was a few months before Kristallnacht.
I was bar mitzvahed September 1938.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] By that time, the German government had shut down some of the synagogues in Germany on the grounds that they caused traffic congestion and that the neighbors complained of the noise and the people coming and going in the street at all hours and what have you.
And they closed about half of them.
You just couldn't open them.
There were no services there.
But they left about half of them intact.
Fortunately, the synagogue that I was bar mitzvahed in was one that was left open.
And I remember that it was filled to the last seat.
It seems to be a well-known phenomenon that when Jews or other people are persecuted, they turn towards religion.

And before the 1930s, there were many agnostic Jews who did not participate in the Jewish life of Berlin, but by 1938, there was not a single Jew in Berlin who did not go to a synagogue or did not in some way participate in the Jewish life.
It was a little orthodox synagogue.
Well, it was modern orthodox.
Say what we call conservative, as I said before.
And it was simply called by the name of the street it was in.
It was-- the street was Passau Strasse.
Passau is a city in Germany, and the street was simply named after the city of Passau.
And the synagogue had a strange location.
It was in the back of an apartment building.
You walked through the apartment house, and there was a big courtyard behind it, and in this courtyard stood the synagogue.
And that's where I was bar mitzvahed.
And we had a rabbi and a cantor, and we had the boys choir.
And all of my relatives that were still living in Berlin came.
And then we had a little bit of a party at home, because Jews were not allowed to go to any restaurants anymore.
It was the end of 1938.
And my mother and my aunts came and helped her and they baked and cooked and things like that.
And we had a traditional lunch.
It was a nice day.
I remember it very well.
Not in my day, no.
No.
Propaganda was everywhere.
It was on the radio every time that you heard either Hitler or Goebbels or Goering or any of the ministers speak, they blamed every problem in Germany on the Jews.

In Germany, as all over Europe, they have these what they call kiosks, which are simply round pillars on which the day's newspaper is posted, also some other posters advertising movies and theater, and these would be full of cartoons showing Jews always big nosed and greedy looking and carrying bags of money strapped to their belt and with some sort of a caption under it, blaming the Jews for this and for that.
And the official slogan-- one of the official slogans-- of the Nazi regime that was plastered all over the city of Berlin was die juden sind unser ungluck, which means that the Jews are our curse.
OK.
Or bad luck.
Actually translated properly, it's bad luck.
And this you saw in every park bench and on every-- it was like graffiti all over town.
So we saw a lot of that.
And, of course, the newspapers, as I said, were full of articles about Jewish store owners exploiting their employees.
And this Jew had been caught with a non-Jewish woman.
And this Jew was sent to jail, and this person was sent to jail.
Though he was not Jewish, he had a Jewish grandmother, which makes him-- which explains the obvious of why he was a criminal, and things like that, beyond any rhyme or reason.
Kristallnacht.
November 9th, 1938.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember it very well.
We woke up in the morning.
My brother and I shared a room, and we heard something that sounded like a crash and we also heard glass shatter.
And it woke us up about, oh, it must have been about 5:30 in the morning.
But we didn't think much of it, because we lived in central Berlin, not for from the theater district, and there were some nightclubs there.
And there was a lot of traffic at night sometimes.

And sometimes there were some drunk drivers, and there were many accidents that happened-- automobile accidents-- and we thought somebody had crashed into a lamppost or into another car, and it was just that.
So we just went back to sleep for another hour, and then when mother woke us up, she said to me go outside and buy some milk.
In those days, you didn't have supermarkets.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Milk was obtained two ways.
It was either delivered to your door or you went to a little store called a creamery that sold milk and cheese.
Jews were not entitled to door-to-door delivery anymore, because the drivers refused to serve Jews.
So fortunately, in the front of our apartment house on the street level, there were several little stores, and one of them was a creamery.
And my mother would every morning send-- we took turns, my brother and I-- send us out.
So I went out it with a little pail that we had-- a little container just for the milk, and I went out to buy a quart of milk.
And when I came out to the street, I saw that there was another shop which belonged to a Jewish neighbor, who was a furrier, that his show window had been smashed.
And I bought the milk, and I went back in, and I said to my mother, I don't know what happened.
We heard a crash this morning and now Mr.
Cohen's window-- storefront-- is all smashed in.
And my mother said, well, it must have been a drunk or some rowdies who threw stones or something.
Just get dressed and go to school and be careful.
And don't stop anywhere to look.
Just go and-- and we went to the corner of the next main street, which was a busy shopping street, and there we saw that all the Jewish stores had been smashed.
And there were big crowds in the street, and there were some police there, but they didn't restrain the crowds.
The crowds were egged on by stormtroopers in their brown uniforms.
And they were looting all the Jewish stores.
So my brother and I got scared, and we ran back home.
We told my mother, and she woke up my father and said, well, something is happening.
This is bad, because there's a big angry crowd out there.
And my mother said you're not going to school.
Just put your books away.
And we stayed in the house, and we listened to the radio.

And it said that there were mobs all over the central part of Berlin and other parts, being angry because this German consular had been shot in the Paris embassy by a Polish Jew and that the German people wanted revenge.
And they were going to kill 100 Jews for every-- for this one, you know.
So my mother and father said this is very dangerous.
I think you better [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not go outside.
In fact, we're going to go down into the cellar, and we're going to stay there during the day.
Down in the basement, they had storage bins, which we called the cellar.
It was a basement of the apartment house.
And we went in there, and we had some wooden crates that contained our Passover dishes.
And behind that, we had a little folding table and some chairs.
And we had prepared that just in case.
So we went in there.
We were very quiet and reading and things, but always very quiet and nothing happened.
So about 4:00 or 5:00 in the afternoon, my brother and I went outside.
My parents said, well, go outside and take a look.
They're not going to bother you, because you're children.
And the apartment house that we lived in was owned by a Jewish person.
Because Jews could not find apartments.
The Gentile house owners would not rent to Jews anymore by 1938.
So we lived in this house, and there were a lot of Jewish families living there because of that.
And it was wintertime.
It was November.
So by about 5:30, it started to get dark.
And then we saw a whole bunch of people carrying torches, coming down our street.
And my brother got very scared, and he ran back into our building and was going to tell my parents.
And I said, well, I'll wait a couple of minutes to see what happens.
I was 13 then.
And this whole mob of people-- there were some stormtroopers, and there were just other civilian people that we-- I didn't know.
They stopped in front of our building, and then some of the stormtroopers went in and some of the other people.
And they were yelling nobody goes in and nobody goes out, and I was out.
I was in the street.
So the people just stood there for about 10 minutes.
And then somebody opened a window upstairs on the third or fourth floor, and they said look out below.
And they started to throw furniture out the windows.
And I knew whose apartment this was, and this was the apartment of Jewish people.
And they were up there with torches and all kinds of things.
What were their names?
Hm?
I beg your pardon?
I don't remember their names, but I know that they were Jewish people because, as I said, a number of Jewish families lived in that building, maybe a dozen or so.
And they threw all their furniture out the window.
And then they went to another apartment, and they did the same thing.

They threw stuff out the window, and people in the street would pick up whatever they found there and, you know, but by the time it landed in the street, there wasn't-- because anything valuable, these people that were in apartments, probably put in their pockets.
So we were afraid that they would do this to my parents' apartment, at least I was.
And I didn't know what to do or where to go.
So I just stayed in the street.
It was dark.
I stayed away from the crowd.
So hid in a doorway on the opposite side of the street.
And after about an hour or two-- maybe it was two hours or so-- they got tired of this, and they went away.
And they went to some other street.
I didn't follow them.
And I didn't know what had happened.
So I went back into the apartment building, and my parents were still in the cellar, and my brother was with them.
And they had been terribly worried about me, because they didn't know what had happened to me.
So I said, well, all is clear outside.
They've gone away, you know.
And so my father said, well, I'm going to see if I can help the people or do something for them or if they need someplace to stay that had been put upon by these people.
But they had already left and gone somewhere else.
So he said, well, I think it's over for now.
And we'll go back to the apartment.
So we went back to the apartment, and we had some dinner.
We didn't put the lights on.
I remember it was dark.
And my mother made some sandwiches and things like that.
And we went to sleep.
And she said, well, tomorrow you have to go to school.
And about 2:00 in the morning, there was a knock on the door.
And there were two men, and they were Gestapo, and they said that my father had to come with them.
And my father said there must be some mistake.
I am German, and I'm a veteran, and you have the wrong person.
You certainly don't want me.
And they, well, are you Harry Sinason, et cetera and so on, and he said yes.
And they said, well, you have to come with us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what happened next, I found out later on, he was taken first to the local police station where there were some other Jews.
And then he was taken to a concentration camp outside of Berlin called Sachsenhausen.
Sachsenhausen was not a death camp or a labor camp.
It was really a concentration camp.
It was a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] collection point for Jews from the greater Berlin area.
I beg your pardon?
I will tell you that story in a moment.
When my father got there, the next morning he became very vocal.
My father had a very strong temper, if not violent, and these people were stepping on something that was very sacrosanct to him.
And he carried on something fierce, that he was German, that he was a veteran, that he was decorated, that he had an Iron Cross, and that no way was he going to submit to these indignities.
And he wanted to talk to the commandant of the camp.
Well, he made such a row, that two of the guards took him to the commandant of the camp.
And now a miracle starts to unfold.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The commandant of the camp, as were many other commandants of these concentration camps, was a retired German army officer, a colonel.
And he questioned my father, what's your problem?
Why are you carrying on like this?
And my father reached into the pocket of his suit and smashed his Iron Cross on the table.
And he said I deserve this.
I risked my life for the [INAUDIBLE] and the Kaiser gave me this.
And I was a-- So the colonel, being an army man, said well where were you during the First World War?
And my father told him, and he said do you know that that was my regiment?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So my father said, well, now very civil and Germans in front of authority become very obedient.
And my father being confronted with this military authority, now he had respect for this man, said, may I ask your name?
And he said, oh, yes.
I do remember.
You were our colonel.
And the colonel dismissed the guards, and he said to my father, what am I going to do with you?
I really don't know what to do with you.
I can't-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you have some urgent business to take care of, right?
And my father said-- and he winked at him-- and my father said, yes, absolutely.
And he says, do you have a family?
He said, yes, I have two small children, and my wife is at home.
And I-- I didn't know I had to leave.
My affairs are not in order.
And the commandant and said, OK, I will give you a 48-hour pass.
And then you must report back here, and he winked at him.
And he let my father go.
And my father took a train back to Berlin, and he came home.
And he says we've got to get out of the country.
No, I-- I don't know.
I don't remember that-- who that was.
You mean the commandant of the camp?
During Kristallnacht, our synagogue was burned, like all the others.
We saw the flames, because we only lived about four blocks away.
But we did not go near it, and there was nothing that anybody could do.
It was not burned very badly because, as I said before, it was right next to an apartment house, and the fire department came to protect the apartment house.
But they let the synagogue burn.
So mostly the roof of the synagogue burned, and it caved in.
But after that, either they extinguished the fire or it just burned itself out.
I don't know, but the walls were left standing.
And I saw it one time after I went back to Berlin after the war.
OK, so what-- in the meantime, of course, my parents had seen the writing on the wall.
And we had made some preparations to leave Germany anyway.
And we wanted to go to the United States, because we had some relatives here.
There's a little side story here.
My maternal grandmother had a young sister, who was a very beautiful girl, and during World War I, they lived in the Rhineland, which was occupied by the American Expeditionary Forces in 1918.
And a nice young Jewish man from The Bronx fell in love with my Great Aunt-- my grandmother's sister-- and she was a war bride.
And he took her back to the United States to The Bronx.
And his name was George Helman.
And that was my aunt-- great aunt Paula.
And through this great aunt being here in the United States-- in those days in the '30s, you needed someone to sponsor you to come to the United States.
And there was a quota of about 5,000 Jews from Germany a year.
And she had sent the necessary paper to us and to Washington.
But on the quota number, we were something like 22,000 something or other, which meant we had to wait about four years.
So now my father had to get out of Germany within 48 hours.

And there was no country in the world that would take Jews without a long waiting period, without having a visa, without having a lot of people in that country that would vouch, say, for them and sponsor them and what have you.
There was one place in the world that Jews could go to without any paper.
All they needed was a ticket, and that was China, the city of Shanghai.
The city of Shanghai in 1939 was already occupied by the Japanese, and apparently the Japanese had established some ammunition factories in Shanghai, but they could not get enough labor to work them.
So they let Jews in, and they put them in a ghetto in Shanghai, and they them work in the ammunition factories.
All this, of course, I found out much later.
But at the time, China and Shanghai was a total unknown to everyone, because in Germany I had never seen a Chinese person.
And I don't know if my parents ever had.
OK.

So my father and my mother scraped together some money very quickly-- sold some jewelry and things-- and bought-- had enough to buy two tickets on a ship that left Germany the next day to go to China.
And they decided that we were going to Shanghai.
And my mother now had to make a terrible decision, because she didn't know what she should do with my brother and myself.
I was 13, my brother was 12.
We-- she could have borrowed enough money to buy two additional tickets to go to China with them.
But on the other hand, she said I don't know what China is.
I don't know what they're going to do to us there.
It certainly looks like we're just going there, and the Japanese are going to kill us because they are the Allies of the Germans.
They had already established what was then called the Axis, Berlin and Tokyo, although the war hadn't started yet.
[SIGHS] Now it happened that one of my father's brothers-- my Uncle Richard-- was living in Paris.
He had left Germany in 1933, because he was the head of the Berlin Grain Exchange, and he was on Nazi's hit list.
As soon as they took power, he was one of the people that was to be arrested.
But he was tipped off.
So by night, he simply went by train to Paris.
In those days it was-- border controls were very loose--
--loose, because the Nazis had just taken over.
So he lived in Paris with his wife and little daughter.

And somebody told my parents that there was a way to smuggle children into France and that the French authorities were very lax about verifying papers and things when it came to children, that they really didn't bother with children and that children could cross into France unmolested.
And my mother said, well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] let's do the following-- I think we're going to our certain deaths, and we'll make a copy of this quota paper that we have.
And if God wants it, we'll all meet in the United States in a couple of years.
But you two are going to go and live with your Uncle Richard in Paris.
Of course, it was terribly difficult for her, but she thought that better than to die in some far off place that they never heard of, we would be safer in Paris.
Because, after all, Paris was a civilized place, and France was a civilized country.
And maybe there would be no war and--
I met this person [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] at the railroad station in Berlin.
I just had a briefcase with me.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my father bought a train ticket from Berlin to Paris.
And he said these people will tell you what to do.
And here's a piece of paper with your Uncle's address on it.
He had no telephone.
You go there, and you tell him what happened to me, and he will understand.
And when we get out of Germany, I will write to him and tell him and take care of everything.
Were you with your brother?
I was not with my brother, because it was considered too dangerous to take two children at the same time.
I was-- I went first, and I went alone.
We came to the border-- French-German border-- and the Gestapo made everybody get out of the train, put all the Jews to one side.
We were all body searched.
They confiscated everything we had, including my little briefcase and my salami sandwich.
I remember that.
My mother had bought a salami to take to my Uncle because apparently he liked salami.
I don't know.
Anyway, they took my salami, which made me very unhappy.
They body searched me, and then they said, well, where are you going?
And I said I'm going to Paris.
Why?
I'm going to live with my Uncle, and they said but you have no papers.
And I said I have my passport-- I had a German passport-- and they said that's not enough.
You have no French visa.
And he went to confer with his superior, and they both came back, and he said, OK, you can go, but if you come back, we have to shoot you.
Didn't mean anything, because by this time, you know, you sort of get numb.
And we got back into the train, and this couple that were taking me, they said, OK, now you crawl under the bench.
You know, the European trains have compartments where three people sit on one side, three people on the other facing each other.
And there's a little door to the compartment.
OK.
